nodes.label: C1.2 Cell Respiration
nodes.description: Cell respiration is the controlled release of energy from organic compounds to produce ATP. This overarching topic is divided into several major interconnected concepts that occur across different cellular compartments.

nodes.label: Glycolysis
nodes.description: Glycolysis is an anaerobic process occurring in the cytoplasm. It breaks down a 6-carbon glucose molecule into two 3-carbon pyruvate molecules.

nodes.label: The Link Reaction
nodes.description: If oxygen is present, pyruvate enters the mitochondrial matrix via transport proteins. Here, it undergoes oxidative decarboxylation. Pyruvate loses a carbon atom as carbon dioxide and is simultaneously oxidized, reducing NAD+ to NADH. The remaining 2-carbon acetyl group binds to Coenzyme A, forming Acetyl CoA.

nodes.label: The Krebs Cycle
nodes.description: Located in the mitochondrial matrix, the Krebs Cycle is a cyclical mechanism of oxidation. Throughout this cycle, decarboxylase enzymes remove carbon dioxide, while dehydrogenase enzymes reduce both NAD+ and FAD into NADH and FADH2. A small amount of ATP is also produced via substrate-level phosphorylation.
nodes.pathwaySteps: Acetyl CoA delivers acetyl group -> Binds with 4C Oxaloacetate -> Forms 6C Citrate -> Oxidative Decarboxylation to 5C -> Oxidative Decarboxylation to 4C -> Regeneration of Oxaloacetate

nodes.label: The Electron Transport Chain
nodes.description: The ETC is a series of trans-membrane protein complexes embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane (cristae). NADH and FADH2 deliver high-energy electrons to these complexes. As electrons are passed down the chain, they lose energy. This energy drives proton pumping, moving hydrogen ions from the matrix into the narrow intermembrane space, building a massive concentration gradient. At the end of the chain, molecular oxygen acts as the final electron acceptor, binding with electrons and free protons to form water, preventing the chain from backing up.

nodes.label: Chemiosmosis
nodes.description: Chemiosmosis is the mechanism that couples the proton gradient to ATP synthesis. Because the inner membrane is impermeable to protons, they must diffuse back into the matrix through a specialized enzyme called ATP Synthase.
nodes.pathwaySteps: Proton Motive Force builds -> Protons flow through ATP Synthase -> Rotor subunit turns -> Conformational change in catalytic knob -> ADP binds with inorganic phosphate -> Massive ATP production

nodes.label: Cytoplasm
nodes.description: The cellular compartment where glycolysis occurs.

nodes.label: Glucose
nodes.description: A 6-carbon organic compound, the starting molecule for glycolysis.

nodes.label: Pyruvate
nodes.description: Two 3-carbon molecules produced from the breakdown of glucose during glycolysis, and an input for the Link Reaction.

nodes.label: Phosphorylation (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: The first step in glycolysis where hexokinase uses two ATP to add phosphate groups to glucose, making it unstable.

nodes.label: Hexokinase
nodes.description: An enzyme that catalyzes the phosphorylation of glucose in glycolysis.

nodes.label: Lysis (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: The second step in glycolysis where hexose bisphosphate splits into two triose phosphate molecules.

nodes.label: Oxidation (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: The third step in glycolysis where triose phosphate loses hydrogen, reducing NAD+ to NADH.

nodes.label: NAD+
nodes.description: Nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide, an electron acceptor reduced to NADH during glycolysis, the link reaction, and the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: NADH
nodes.description: Reduced form of NAD+, carrying high-energy electrons to the Electron Transport Chain.

nodes.label: ATP Formation (Glycolysis)
nodes.description: The final step in glycolysis where enzymes transfer phosphate groups directly to ADP, generating a net yield of two ATP via substrate-level phosphorylation.

nodes.label: ADP
nodes.description: Adenosine diphosphate, a precursor for ATP synthesis.

nodes.label: ATP
nodes.description: Adenosine triphosphate, the primary energy currency of the cell, produced in various stages of cell respiration.

nodes.label: Substrate-level phosphorylation
nodes.description: A type of ATP synthesis where a phosphate group is directly transferred from a substrate molecule to ADP, occurring in glycolysis and the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: Mitochondrial matrix
nodes.description: The innermost compartment of the mitochondrion, where the link reaction and Krebs cycle occur.

nodes.label: Transport proteins (Pyruvate)
nodes.description: Proteins embedded in the mitochondrial membrane that facilitate pyruvate entry into the matrix.

nodes.label: Oxidative decarboxylation (Link Reaction)
nodes.description: A process in the link reaction where pyruvate loses a carbon atom as carbon dioxide and is simultaneously oxidized, reducing NAD+ to NADH.

nodes.label: Carbon dioxide
nodes.description: A waste product, a carbon atom removed during oxidative decarboxylation in the link reaction and the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: Acetyl group
nodes.description: The remaining 2-carbon molecule after pyruvate undergoes decarboxylation in the link reaction.

nodes.label: Coenzyme A
nodes.description: A coenzyme that binds to the acetyl group to form Acetyl CoA in the link reaction.

nodes.label: Acetyl CoA
nodes.description: The molecule formed when the acetyl group binds to Coenzyme A, entering the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: Oxaloacetate (4C)
nodes.description: A 4-carbon molecule that combines with Acetyl CoA to start the Krebs cycle, and is regenerated at the end.

nodes.label: Citrate (6C)
nodes.description: A 6-carbon molecule formed from Acetyl CoA and oxaloacetate, the first intermediate of the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: Decarboxylase enzymes
nodes.description: Enzymes that remove carbon dioxide molecules during the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: Dehydrogenase enzymes
nodes.description: Enzymes that remove hydrogen atoms, reducing NAD+ and FAD during the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: FAD
nodes.description: Flavin adenine dinucleotide, an electron acceptor reduced to FADH2 during the Krebs cycle.

nodes.label: FADH2
nodes.description: Reduced form of FAD, carrying high-energy electrons to the Electron Transport Chain.

nodes.label: Trans-membrane protein complexes (ETC)
nodes.description: A series of protein complexes embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane that make up the Electron Transport Chain.

nodes.label: Inner mitochondrial membrane (cristae)
nodes.description: The folded inner membrane of the mitochondrion where the ETC and ATP synthase are located, increasing surface area.

nodes.label: Electrons
nodes.description: High-energy electrons delivered by NADH and FADH2, passed down the Electron Transport Chain.

nodes.label: Proton pumping
nodes.description: The active transport of hydrogen ions from the mitochondrial matrix into the intermembrane space, driven by electron energy in the ETC.

nodes.label: Hydrogen ions (protons)
nodes.description: Ions pumped into the intermembrane space by the ETC, creating a concentration gradient for chemiosmosis.

nodes.label: Intermembrane space
nodes.description: The narrow space between the inner and outer mitochondrial membranes where protons accumulate, creating the proton gradient.

nodes.label: Molecular oxygen (O2)
nodes.description: The final electron acceptor at the end of the ETC, binding with electrons and free protons to form water.

nodes.label: Water (ETC product)
nodes.description: A product formed at the end of the ETC when oxygen accepts electrons and protons.

nodes.label: Final electron acceptor
nodes.description: The role of molecular oxygen in the ETC, crucial for preventing the chain from backing up.

nodes.label: Proton gradient
nodes.description: The concentration difference of protons across the inner mitochondrial membrane, built by the ETC and utilized by chemiosmosis.

nodes.label: ATP Synthase
nodes.description: A specialized enzyme complex embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane that uses the flow of protons (proton motive force) to synthesize ATP during chemiosmosis.

nodes.label: Proton Motive Force
nodes.description: The electrochemical potential energy stored in the proton gradient, which drives ATP synthesis via ATP Synthase.

nodes.label: Rotor subunit (ATP Synthase)
nodes.description: A component of ATP Synthase that turns as protons flow through it, leading to conformational changes.

nodes.label: Catalytic knob (ATP Synthase)
nodes.description: A part of ATP Synthase that undergoes conformational changes triggered by the rotor subunit's movement, leading to ATP synthesis from ADP and inorganic phosphate.

nodes.label: Inorganic phosphate (Pi)
nodes.description: A molecule that combines with ADP to form ATP during chemiosmosis.

nodes.label: Massive ATP production
nodes.description: The primary outcome of chemiosmosis, responsible for generating a large quantity of ATP using the proton motive force.
